title: Swirling flow and spray atomization interactions in a swirl cup airblast fuel
text:
Swirling flow and spray atomization interactions in a swirl cup airblast fuel 
injector: Venturi outlet angle
Yushuai Liu a,c,d,*, Chuanyu Fang b, Shaolin Wang a,c,d,*,  
Cunxi Liu a,c,d,*, Yong Mu a,c,d,e, Gang Xu a,c,d
a Laboratory of Light-Duty Gas-Turbine, Institute of Engineering Thermophysics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, China
b Beijing Institute of Tracking and Telecommunication Technology, Beijing 100094, China
c University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China
d National Key Laboratory of Science and Technology on Advanced Light-duty Gas-turbine, Beijing 100190, China
e Qingdao Institute of Aeronautical Technology, Qingdao 266400 Shandong, China
A R T I C L E  I N F O
Keywords:
Phase-Doppler Particle Analyzer
Particle Imaging Velocimetry
Sauter Mean Diameter
High speed imaging
Weber number
A B S T R A C T
Swirl cup airblast fuel injectors are critical components in modern low-emission gas turbine combustors. Un­
derstanding the underlying physics of the interaction between fuel spray and complex swirling airflow is crucial 
for optimizing injector performance. This study investigates the influence of Venturi outlet angles (15◦, 25◦, and 
35◦) on airflow field and spray atomization dynamics. Advanced optical diagnostics, including high-speed 
shadowgraph, Phase Doppler Particle Analyzer (PDPA), Particle Imaging Velocimetry (PIV), and Planar Mie 
scattering (PMie), were employed to quantify flow-spray interactions under controlled fuel flow rates (2.0–4.0 
kg/h) and 3 % relative air pressure drop. Results demonstrate that the Venturi outlet angle significantly mod­
ulates primary atomization. Increasing the angle from 15◦to 35◦reduces liquid film length by 69.2 % due to 
enhanced gas–liquid shear stress. Moreover, larger angles amplify central toroidal recirculation zone (CTRZ) 
reverse velocity (−1.2 to −6.8 m/s), intensifying droplet entrainment and reducing Sauter Mean Diameter (SMD) 
by 30.9 %. These findings highlight that Venturi angles > 25◦optimize atomization by balancing shear-driven 
breakup and recirculation-enhanced mixing, providing critical insights for designing fuel injectors with 
improved combustion stability and emission performance.
1. Introduction
Modern gas turbine combustors increasingly adopt lean combustion 
strategies (e.g., Lean Direct Injection (LDI) and Lean Premixed perva­
porated (LPP)) to meet stringent emission regulations [1]. Currently, 
swirl cup airblast fuel injectors have become predominant in gas turbine 
combustors, due to their superior atomization and homogeneous 
fuel–air mixing capabilities before combustion [2,3]. Establishing a 
detailed physical understanding of the fluid dynamics in the swirl cup 
airblast fuel injectors, particularly the breakup of liquid sheets and the 
formation of sprays in swirling flows, is crucial for advancing the 
development of next-generation, low-emission gas turbine combustors 
[4].
The atomization process within swirl cup airblast fuel injectors in­
volves multiscale physical phenomena spanning from the near field 
(primary atomization) to far field (secondary atomization) [5]. This 
complex sequence includes: (1) liquid sheet formation at the pressure- 
swirl atomizer [6], (2) spray-wall interactions and film formation 
[7,8], (3) rim destabilization through air shear [7], (4) primary droplet 
generation via Rayleigh-Taylor instabilities [9] (5) subsequent droplet 
fragmentation through aerodynamic secondary breakup [10]. (6) Sec­
ondary breakup, drop collisions and coalescence [11,12], (7) spray in­
teractions with turbulent eddies, cluster formation, drop heat transfer 
and evaporation [13]. Recent studies have systematically examined 
critical operational and geometric parameters influencing spray dy­
namics. Key operational factors include including airflow velocity[14] 
and liquid fuel mass flow rate [15],as well as fuel injector geometric 
parameters including geometric swirl number (SN) [16], swirling flow 
directions [17], Venturi [18], flare [19], swirler angle [20], etc.
Candel et al. [21] Ying et al. [22] Jeng et al. [23] investigated the 
effects of the operation parameters on the spray characteristics. The 
studies concluded that swirling flow is characterized by the Central 
* Corresponding authors at: Institute of Engineering Thermophysics Chinese Academy of Science, Beijing 100190, China.
E-mail addresses: liuyushuai@iet.cn (Y. Liu), wangshaolin@iet.cn (S. Wang), liucunxi@iet.cn (C. Liu). 
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect

title: Swirling flow and spray atomization interactions in a swirl cup airblast fuel
text:
Swirling flow and spray atomization interactions in a swirl cup airblast fuel 
injector: Venturi outlet angle
Yushuai Liu a,c,d,*, Chuanyu Fang b, Shaolin Wang a,c,d,*,  
Cunxi Liu a,c,d,*, Yong Mu a,c,d,e, Gang Xu a,c,d
a Laboratory of Light-Duty Gas-Turbine, Institute of Engineering Thermophysics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, China
b Beijing Institute of Tracking and Telecommunication Technology, Beijing 100094, China
c University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China
d National Key Laboratory of Science and Technology on Advanced Light-duty Gas-turbine, Beijing 100190, China
e Qingdao Institute of Aeronautical Technology, Qingdao 266400 Shandong, China
A R T I C L E  I N F O
Keywords:
Phase-Doppler Particle Analyzer
Particle Imaging Velocimetry
Sauter Mean Diameter
High speed imaging
Weber number
A B S T R A C T
Swirl cup airblast fuel injectors are critical components in modern low-emission gas turbine combustors. Un­

for optimizing injector performance. This study investigates the influence of Venturi outlet angles (15◦, 25◦, and

scattering (PMie), were employed to quantify flow-spray interactions under controlled fuel flow rates (2.0–4.0

enhanced gas–liquid shear stress. Moreover, larger angles amplify central toroidal recirculation zone (CTRZ) 
reverse velocity (−1.2 to −6.8 m/s), intensifying droplet entrainment and reducing Sauter Mean Diameter (SMD)

title: Swirling flow and spray atomization interactions in a swirl cup airblast fuel
text:
Experimental Thermal and Fluid Science
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/etfs
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.expthermflusci.2025.111622
Received 27 April 2025; Received in revised form 27 August 2025; Accepted 18 September 2025  
Experimental Thermal and Fluid Science 171 (2026) 111622 
Available online 22 September 2025 
0894-1777/© 2025 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies. 

Toroidal Recirculation Zone (CTRZ). The dimensions of the CTRZ are 
regarded as a crucial factor in determining the topological structure of 
the spray. Due to the presence of the CTRZ, there is a significant gradient 
in velocities between liquid droplets and the gas flow, leading to notable 
momentum exchange between the airflow and spray droplets. All 
droplets of various diameters move downstream within the spray cone, 
with smaller droplets recirculating upstream within the spray cone, and 
larger droplets decelerating downstream. Bokhart et al. [24,25] focused 
their research on the spray characteristics of different fuels under high- 
temperature and high-pressure conditions. The characteristics studied 
included droplet size and diameter. The results showed that under high- 
temperature and high-pressure conditions, the injection pressure has 
little effect on droplets size.
Regarding the influence of injector geometric parameters, numerous 
scholars have conducted extensive research. Fu et al. [26] studied the 
effect of confinement on swirling flow within a swirl cup. Their findings 
suggest that confinement affects the dimensions and intensity of the 
recirculation zone. Billant et al. [27] explored the correlation between 
vortex breakdown and the SN. Their study revealed that vortex break­
down occurred when the SN reached a critical threshold. Hadef et al. 
[28] examined the influence of swirling flow directions, particularly the 
effects of co-swirl and counter-swirl on droplet behavior. The research 
shows that the counter-swirler configurations promote a higher level of 
turbulence, which enhances fuel–air mixing and leads to a more even 
spray distribution. Kumar et al. [29] studied the impact of co-swirling 
and counter-swirling flows on droplet distribution and size, finding 
that the shear forces between the primary and secondary swirling flows 
are more pronounced in the counter-swirling case, thereby enhancing 
secondary atomization and improving spray uniformity. Merkle et al. 
[30] investigated how co-swirling and counter-swirling flows affect the 
lean stability limits. The findings suggest that the counter-swirler 
configuration enables a higher thermal load and a broader operational 
range compared to the co-swirler configurations. Rajamanickam et al. 
[31] investigated the effects of swirling flow direction and flare angles 
on droplet behavior and the flow field. Their study demonstrates that 
variations in swirling flow direction and flare design significantly alter 
the flow field’s topological structure and subsequent dynamics, noting a 
decreased axial extent of the CTRZ with counter-rotating swirls, sug­
gesting a reduction in tangential velocity. Lin et al. [32] explored the 
effect of the Venturi on non-reactive flows, concluding that a Venturi 
with reduced swirling strength can effectively mitigate combustion in­
stabilities in lean fuel regions. Ateshkadi et al. [33] used Planar Laser- 
Induced Fluorescence (PLIF) to assess the Venturi’s impact on atomi­
zation and fuel distribution, revealing that the Venturi’s structure plays 
a crucial role in the liquid film’s breakup process and the subsequent 
droplet distribution, with the Venturi confining the spray to a more 
focused area than cases without it. Jeung et al.[34] investigated the 
influence of the Venturi tip angle on the flow field and atomization 
process. Their research indicates that the Venturi tip angle has a pro­
nounced effect on the position and form of both internal and external 
recirculation zones, which in turn impacts the spray breakup charac­
teristics. A converging Venturi tip angle intensifies the velocity gradient 
between the primary and secondary swirlers, resulting in a decrease in 
the Sauter Mean Diameter (SMD). Fang et al.[35] applied the BP neural 
network to predict SMD as a function of geometry, condition parame­
ters. The result shows that the BP neural network is a novel and effective 
way to predict the SMD of droplets generated by a swirl cup airblast fuel 
injector. Han et al.[36] found that adjusting the fuel injector position 
modifies the fuel spray pattern’s structure, with moving the injector 
downstream lessening the aerodynamic influence on the nozzle and 
leading to an increase in SMD at the spray center.
The poor atomization quality adversely affects kernel generation and 
flame propagation, ultimately leading to deteriorated ignition perfor­
mance. [37,38] The flame propagation speed in the annular combustor 
is influenced by atomization quality and droplet size distribution. As 
atomization quality improves, it becomes easier for ignition to 
propagate between adjacent injectors. This effect is particularly pro­
nounced near the lean blowout limits[39]. In the meantime, an over 
misty spray can intensely increase the flame speed, which increases NOx 
emission and narrows the combustor’s operating equivalence ratio 
range. Liu et al.[40] examined the influence of spray characteristics on 
flame structures and combustion stability, highlighting the critical role 
of spray quality in maintaining combustion stability during ignition and 
near lean blow-out (LBO) conditions. Colby et al.[41] studied swirl cup 
injection and observed that larger droplets tend to traverse the main 
recirculation region rapidly with short residence times, whereas smaller 
droplets experience longer residence times, contributing to the stabili­
zation of the lean flame.
Despite numerous research have explored the flow field, spray 
characteristics, and combustion performance of swirl cup airblast in­
jectors [14–34]. A comprehensive understanding between Venturi 
structures and complex swirling fields remains elusive. Prior works 
primarily focused on swirl number [16], flow direction [17,21–24], and 
flare angles [19,24]. Venturi outlet angle – a key geometric control 
parameter for shear layer dynamics – remains quantitatively unchar­
acterized. While Jeung et al. [34] noted its influence on recirculation 
zones, systematic quantification of its impact on liquid film breakup (e. 
g., thickness, length) and droplet entrainment is absent. Moreover 
Existing studies [19,26,33] relied on isolated measurements (e.g., PLIF 
for fuel distribution or PDPA for droplet size). No prior work integrates 
simultaneous diagnostics for film dynamics (shadowgraph), flow fields 
(PIV), and droplet statistics (PDPA/PMie) – essential for decoupling 
primary/secondary atomization mechanisms modulated by Venturi 
angles.
To address this gap, the current study utilized an array of advanced 
laser-based diagnostic techniques, including Phase Doppler Particle 
Analyzer (PDPA), high-speed Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV), and 
high-speed Planar Mie scattering (PMie), high-speed shadowgraph, 
combined with three Venturi outlet angles (15◦, 25◦, and 35◦) configu­
rations to quantify key parameters such as SMD, droplet velocity, air 
flow velocity, and spray angle, film thickness. The research aimed to 
discern the effects of the Venturi outlet angles on spray characteristics 
and the dynamics of droplets under different the Venturi outlet angles. 
This further aimed to refine the swirl-spray coupling model and provide 
engineering guidance for optimizing low-emission combustion systems.
The structure of the paper is as follows: Section II details the geo­
metric parameters of the swirl cup airblast fuel injector and the exper­
imental methodology, including the diagnostic tools employed. Section 
III pre

little effect on droplets size.

title: Swirling flow and spray atomization interactions in a swirl cup airblast fuel
text:
Venturi lip shears this film into ligaments, initiating atomization. In this 
study three Venturi outlet angles (θ = 15◦, 25◦, and 35◦) were used to 
discern the effects on spray performance.
A custom-designed atomization test rig was employed in this study, 
as also depicted in Fig. 1. The test rig includes three key components, 
which are gas supply, liquid supply, and data acquisition. The test uti­
lized dried compressed air at room temperature, supplied at 6 bar to the 
fuel injectors. The flow rate of the air was regulated by a mass flow 
controller (0–300 lpm, uncertainty: ±2%). For the calculating the air 
pressure drop, the inlet total pressure and outlet static pressure of the 
fuel injectors under test were measured using a differential pressure 
sensor (range: 0–10 psi, uncertainty: ±0.05 %). Based on our previous 
research [42], for the injectors used in this study, the effect of air 
pressure drop on droplet size distribution is minimal. Hence, a constant 
relative air pressure drop (ΔPa = 3 %) was maintained throughout the 
experiments. Chinese aviation kerosene RP-3[43], used as the working 
fluid, was metered at 2.0 kg /h and 4.0 kg/h by a mass flow controller 
(range: 0–20 kg/h, uncertainty: ± 0.1 %). Two pressure transducers (0 – 
Fig. 1. Scheme of the atomization test rig, swirl cup airblast fuel injector,
Fig. 2. PIV, PMie, Shadowgraph measuring area, and PDPA measuring position.
Y. Liu et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
Experimental Thermal and Fluid Science 171 (2026) 111622 
3

title: Swirling flow and spray atomization interactions in a swirl cup airblast fuel
text:
0.6 MPa, uncertainty: ± 0.1 %) were utilized to monitor the fuel static 
pressure at the inlet of the fuel injectors for cross-verification. A 
LabVIEW-based inhouse code was used to control the mass flow con­
trollers for adjusting the fuel mass flow rate (mf) and relative air pres­
sure drop. All the instruments were carefully zeroed before every 
experiment.
2.2. Optical diagnostic methods
Four advanced laser-based techniques (PDPA, High-speed PIV, High- 
speed shadowgraph, and High-speed PMie) were employed to quantify 
spray characteristics and flow dynamics.
Fig. 2 illustrates the measurement areas for PIV, PMie, and shad­
owgraph techniques, as well as the positions measured by PDPA. The 
PIV and PMie measurement areas are two identical rectangular regions 
of 80.0 × 70.0 mm2, which are symmetrical about the swirl cup airblast 
fuel injector center line. The high-speed shadowgraph technique was 
employed to capture the overall breakup within the Venturi with a field 
of view encompassing 16.0 × 7.0 mm2. Additionally, for the breakup at 
the edge of the Venturi, a more focused field of view was utilized, 
measuring 7.65 × 4.2 mm2, respectively. In the PDPA measurements, 
the radial distribution of the spray is sampled at 2 mm intervals, until 
reached the radial boundary. These measurements are carried out at 
eight axial positions (z = 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 mm). Taking the 
outer swirler exit diameter, d=15 mm as the normalization parameter, 
the radial and axial positions are in the range ofr/d = 0.00 ~ 1.33 in the 
radial direction andz/d = 0.33 ~ 4.00 in the vertical direction.
PIV and PMie systems were used to capture the 2-D flow field ve­
locity distribution and spray angle of the swirl cup airblast fuel injector 
configuration. 527 nm dual-pulsed laser (2000 Hz, 30mJ/pulse) with a 
cylindrical lens system (1 mm waist thickness) was used to illuminate 
the PIV flow tracing particle and fuel droplet. The scattered light from 
the PIV flow tracing

title: Swirling flow and spray atomization interactions in a swirl cup airblast fuel
text:
for three cases under two different fuel flow rates. A consistent obser­
vation across all cases is the occurrence of film breakup within a short 
distance (z/d = 0.3) from the Venturi outlet. When comparing the higher 
fuel flow rate (4.0 kg/h) and lower fuel flow rate (2.0 kg/h) within the 
same case, the breakup length remains relatively constant. However, a 
significant increase in the number of films and ligaments at the Venturi 
edge is evident at the higher flow rate, indicating that higher flow rates 
lead to a thicker liquid film accumulating at the edge. Comparing 
different cases at the same fuel flow rate reveals that the film breakup 
length decreases with increasing Venturi outlet angle. Specifically, 
compared to Case 1, Case 3 with a larger angle enhances gas–liquid 
shear. This enhanced shear correlates with a substantial reduction in 
film breakup length (by 50 %, z/d = 0.2 to z/d = 0.1, as detailed in 
Fig. 4), contributing to more efficient breakup. This suggests that the 
Venturi outlet angle plays a crucial role in determining the stability and 
breakup of the liquid film.
To complement the overall breakup length observations in Fig. 4, 
Fig. 5 provides high-resolution zoomed-in views of the film breakup 
front (marked by red dashed boxes in Fig. 2). This focused imaging, 
enabled by the 2.0 × telecentric lens of the shadowgraph system, reveals 
the transient thickness evolution of the liquid film as it thins and frag­
ments—details that are not resolvable in the larger field of view of Fig. 4.
In this study, the liquid thickness is quantified as the maximum 
vertical thickness of the liquid film at the exact location of initial frac­
ture. Thickness data were derived through a two-step process: (1) image 
grayscale calibration to convert pixel intensity to physical dimensions, 
and (2) edge detection algorithms to precisely identify the film bound­
aries at the breakup initiation point. This method ensures consistent 
quantification of breakup

title: Swirling flow and spray atomization interactions in a swirl cup airblast fuel
text:
leading to an increase in liquid film thickness. Conversely, an increase in 
the Venturi outlet angle results in a corresponding decrease in the liquid 
film thickness at the edge. Notably, the liquid film thickness for Case 1 
(θ = 15◦) is significantly greater than that for both Case 2 (θ = 25◦) and 
Case 3 (θ = 35◦). Specifically, increasing the Venturi outlet angle from 
15◦to 35◦reduces the liquid film thickness by 69.2 %, from 938 μm to 
289 μm, as detailed in this figure.
The spray spatial distribution with the superimposed flow field ve­
locity vector maps are shown in Fig. 6 and Fig. 8. The length and di­
rection of the vectors represent the magnitude and direction of the flow 
field velocity, respectively. The green line indicates the spray boundary, 
defined in this study as the radial position encompassing 90 % of the 
cumulative Mie-scattering intensity from the spray center outward. 
Spray angle serves as a crucial metric for evaluating spray dispersion, 
droplet spatial distribution, and overall injector performance. In this 
study, it is defined as the angle formed between the Venturi tip and this 
spray boundary.
Fig. 6 illustrates that at low fuel flow rates (mf = 2.0kg/h), For all 
three configurations, the concentration distribution exhibits consistent 
characteristics: peak intensity occurs at the centerline (r/d = 0), indi­
cating droplet accumulation within the CTRZ, while edge regions (|r/d|
> 0.5) show sharp intensity reduction due to SJZ entrainment. This 
central concentration peak arises from two factors: (1) poor atomization 
at low fuel flow rates allows larger droplets to exit the Venturi outlet 
without full breakup, and (2) high shear stress at the Venturi lip gen­
erates fine droplets that follow gas-phase streamlines. Centrifugal forces 
drive droplet migration outward, but this is counteracted by CTRZ 
reverse flow, enhancing droplet retention in the central region and 
strengthening the PMie signal. As the spray develops axially,

title: Swirling flow and spray atomization interactions in a swirl cup airblast fuel
text:
The PMie intensity signals extracted from the near-swirler region 
(z/d = 0.33) and the downstream development region (z/d = 1.33) in 
Fig. 6 are extracted and presented in Fig. 7. Analysis of Fig. 7 reveals that 
the Case1 (θ = 15◦) exhibits the weakest PMie signal intensity. Its peak 
intensity is 46 % lower than that of the Case 3 (θ = 35◦). This significant 
difference correlates with the PIV flow field mappings (as shown in 
Fig. 3), which show that Case 3 achieves a 65 % higher centerline 
reverse flow velocity within the CTRZ. The enhanced reverse velocity 
indicates stronger airflow entrainment. For the Case 3, this intensified 
recirculation is a key factor contributing to the increased PMie signal 
intensity, likely by promoting droplet retention and concentration in the 
central region. As the spray progresses downstream, centrifugally driven 
fine droplets migrate outward, forming distinct outer peaks at 
approximately |r/d| ≈0.4. However, the signal intensity at these outer 
peaks decreases rapidly with axial distance. Among the three configu­
rations, the weakest overall PMie intensity is consistently observed for 
Case 1. This observation, considered alongside the Sauter Mean Diam­
eter (SMD) results presented in Fig. 10, can be attributed to the poorer 
atomization quality associated with the smaller Venturi angle in Case 1. 
Poorer atomization results in fewer fine droplets generated downstream 
of the swirler, leading directly to the weaker PMie signal intensity 
measured for this configuration.
As the fuel flow rate increases to mf = 4.0kg/h, the fuel distribution 
and spray angle exhibit only minor variations across different Venturi 
configurations, as depicted in Fig. 8. This phenomenon arises primarily 
due to the decreased momentum ratio between the airflow and fuel 
Fig. 6. Spray distributions with superimposed flow field velocity vector, all the cases were operated at ΔPa = 3%, mf = 2.0kg/h.
Fig. 7. PMie intensity signals extracted from the

title: Swirling flow and spray atomization interactions in a swirl cup airblast fuel
text:
droplets at the higher fuel flow rate. This reduction diminishes the air­
flow’s capacity to effectively transport and control the droplets aero­
dynamically. Furthermore, the spray angle of the pressure-swirl 
atomizer widens with increasing fuel flow rate, directing a greater 
proportion of fuel towards the Venturi wall. This results in the formation 
of thicker liquid films. These films accumulate at the Venturi lip, locally 
increasing the air-to-fuel ratio (ALR) at the edge. This elevated ALR 
hinders the aerodynamic breakup of the liquid film, leading to pro­
nounced aggregation of liquid film at the Venturi edge. Consequently, 
the PMie signal intensity becomes significantly stronger at the Venturi 
edge due to the increased concentration of liquid mass and larger 
structures present there. Additionally, Case 1 (θ = 15◦) exhibits the most 
significant film aggregation. This is attributed to the reduced shear force 
exerted by the swirl flow at the Venturi lip associated with the smaller 
angle, as evidenced by the thicker films measured for Case 1 in Fig. 5. 
This reduced shear further exacerbates poor atomization of the liquid 
film, contributing substantially to the observed film accumulation at the 
edge, as clearly shown in Fig. 8(a).
The PMie intensity signals extracted from the near-swirler region 
(z/d = 0.33) and the downstream development region (z/d = 1.33), as 
identified in Fig. 7, are systematically presented in Fig. 9 for compara­
tive analysis.
Analysis of Fig. 9(a) reveals a distinct M−shaped distribution of fuel 
PMie signals in the near-swirler region. A prominent intensity peak is 
consistently observed at the right Venturi outlet position (r/d = 0.5), 
significantly exceeding the signal intensity measured at the symmetrical 
Fig. 8. Spray distributions with superimposed flow field velocity vector, all the cases were operated at ΔPa = 3%, mf = 4.0kg/h.
Fig. 9. PMie intensity signals extracted from the regions near the swirler (z/d = 0.33) a

title: Swirling flow and spray atomization interactions in a swirl cup airblast fuel
text:
left outlet position (r/d = -0.5). This observed asymmetry stems from 
two primary factors: (1) fuel accumulation occurring at the Venturi 
outlet edge, and (2) the experimental configuration where the laser 
source enters from the right side. This laser path results in attenuation of 
the incident beam intensity after scattering by the denser fuel concen­
tration on the right side, further accentuating the asymmetry in the 
detected signal.
Among the three Venturi configurations, the maximum PMie in­
tensity at r/d = 0.5 is observed for the Case 1. This heightened intensity 
correlates directly with the significantly thicker liquid film measured for 
Case 1 in Fig. 5. The correlation suggests that inadequate film breakup at 
the Venturi lip, particularly for the smaller 15◦angle, leads to sub­
stantial fuel accumulation at the outlet edge, thereby increasing the 
local PMie signal intensity on both sides (r/d = ±0.5).
When comparing the PMie intensity specifically at the Venturi outlet 
positions (r/d = ±0.5) and the swirler centerline (r/d = 0) across all 
three cases in the near-swirler region (Fig. 9(a)), the intensity levels 
remain relatively constant. This consistency is attributed to the fixed 
relative air pressure drop (ΔPa = 3 %) maintained during the experi­
ments. This controlled condition ensures a similar strength of the 
recirculation zone’s droplet entrainment capability, resulting in mini­
mal variation in PMie intensity at these specific locations, regardless of 
the Venturi angle or fuel flow rate within the tested range.
Fig. 9(b) presents the PMie intensity signals in the downstream 
development region (z/d = 1.33). Here, a significant contrast emerges: 
Case 3 exhibits substantially higher overall PMie intensity compared to 
both Case 1 and Case 2. This downstream enhancement suggests that the 
larger Venturi outlet angle in Case 3 promotes enhanced atomization 
efficiency and yields a higher concentration of fuel droplets in the far 
field, c

title: Swirling flow and spray atomization interactions in a swirl cup airblast fuel
text:
attributed to the increased Venturi wall area enhancing aerodynamic 
shear and film breakup efficiency. However, the Venturi angle shows 
minimal impact on SMD at the centerline (r/d = 0).
Elevating the fuel flow rate transforms the SMD profile from W- 
shaped to U-shaped. A sharp decrease in SMD occurs at the centerline 
(r/d = 0), now exhibiting the minimum values, while maximum SMD 
shifts to the spray periphery (|r/d| > 0.5). As the spray develops 
downstream (increasing z/d), SMD values at the edge decrease signifi­
cantly. This distribution shift is linked to higher fuel flow rates: 
increased fuel impingement on the Venturi wall forms thicker films at 
the edge, resulting in poorer local atomization and larger droplets. These 
larger droplets, possessing higher inertia, migrate centrifugally towards 
the spray edge. Simultaneously, finer droplets remain entrained near the 
center, leading to lower central SMD and higher edge SMD. The 
persistence of higher SMD at the spray edge under high flow rates, 
despite downstream development, underscores the challenge of atom­
izing the fuel accumulating at the Venturi lip under these conditions. 
The centrifugal migration of larger droplets dominates the spatial dis­
tribution in this regime.
3.3. Limitations and Future work
While this study clarifies the role of Venturi outlet angles in modu­
lating spray atomization dynamics, it has inherent limitations that 
should be acknowledged.
First, the experimental scope is currently focused on the liquid film 
breakup process near the Venturi outlet. Upstream stages of the liquid 
film evolution chain—including droplet impingement on the Venturi 
wall, initial film formation, and its transient motion along the wall­
—remain insufficiently explored. These upstream processes directly in­
fluence film thickness and stability at the outlet, and their interplay with 
Venturi angles requires systematic investigation to fully understand at­
omization mechanisms. Second, all experi

title: Swirling flow and spray atomization interactions in a swirl cup airblast fuel
text:
methods. Various sophisticated laser-based diagnostics, including PDPA, 
high-speed PMie, high-speed PIV and high-speed shadowgraph were 
employed to measure critical parameters such as, airflow velocity, film 
breakup length, fuel distribution, and droplet Sauter Mean Diameter 
(SMD). This study elucidates the critical role of Venturi outlet angles in 
modulating spray atomization dynamics within swirl cup airblast fuel 
injectors. The key findings can be summarized as follows:
Increasing the Venturi outlet angle from 15◦to 35◦significantly 
enhanced gas–liquid shear stress, reducing the liquid film length by 69.2 
% and improving primary atomization.
Larger Venturi angles amplified the reverse velocity of the central 
toroidal recirculation zone (CTRZ) from −
1.2 m/s to −
6.8 m/s, 
intensifying droplet entrainment and secondary breakup through 
enhanced turbulence.
Optimal atomization performance (30.9 % reduction in Sauter Mean 
Diameter, SMD) was achieved at angles > 25◦, demonstrating a critical 
balance between shear-driven liquid film breakup and recirculation- 
driven droplet dispersion.
These findings highlight Venturi outlet angle as a critical geometric 
parameter for optimizing injector design, providing actionable insights 
to improve combustion stability and emission performance in low- 
emission gas turbine combustors.

gation, Formal analysis. Chuanyu Fang: Writing – review & editing, 
Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. 
Shaolin Wang: Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, 
Formal analysis. Cunxi Liu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – 
original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Meth­
odology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Yong Mu: Supervision, Meth­
odology. Gang Xu: Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, 
Investigation, Funding acquisition.

interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence 
the work reported in this paper.

ence Foundation of China (No. 52106188).
Data availability
Data will be made available on request.
